description:
You are acting as the Site-Reliability Engineer on-call.  
    Management has approved a very small firewall change window and you must
    prepare everything so that another operator can copy-paste the commands
    without thinking.  
    Your job is ONLY to stage the change, not to run it (you do not have
    root-level access in this environment).

    1. Create a Bash script called
       /home/user/firewall_update.sh that contains EXACTLY the
       following three, and only these three, non-comment, non-blank lines
       (in this order):

           iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22   -j ACCEPT
           iptables -A INPUT -p icmp             -j ACCEPT
           iptables -A INPUT                    -j DROP

       • The script must start with the standard she-bang line  
         (#!/bin/bash) on its very first line.  
       • You may add comments, but they must start with “#”.
       • After you create the file, make it executable by its owner.

    2. Produce a simple “change-request evidence” log that the automated
       audit robot will later ingest:

       • File path: /home/user/firewall_config.log  
       • The log must contain exactly four lines (end each line with a single
         newline character “\n”, no extra blank lines at the end):
            LINE 1: SHA256:<hash_of_firewall_update.sh>
                    (Replace <hash_of_firewall_update.sh> with the lowercase
                     64-character hexadecimal SHA-256 digest of the final
                     /home/user/firewall_update.sh file.)  
            LINE 2: COMMANDS:3
                    (The literal text “COMMANDS:3” verifying there are exactly
                     three firewall commands.)  
            LINE 3: ICMP:ALLOWED
                    (Literally this string confirming the ICMP rule.)  
            LINE 4: DEFAULT:DROP
                    (Literally this string confirming the default policy.)  

         Example of a correctly formatted log (hash value differs):

            SHA256:daf0a6c152a1a16b7eea7e03136f0e5772a79756e4bb6f5e0d9d149f14e1f1b5
            COMMANDS:3
            ICMP:ALLOWED
            DEFAULT:DROP

    3. Verification tips (do NOT include in the log):
       • Compute the digest with: sha256sum /home/user/firewall_update.sh
       • Remember to trim the filename that sha256sum prints; keep only the
         64-character hash.
       • Count only non-blank & non-comment lines when checking that the
         script contains exactly three firewall commands.

    The automated grader will check the following:
       • /home/user/firewall_update.sh exists, is executable, and its
         non-comment commands match exactly the three lines given above,
         in the specified order.
       • /home/user/firewall_config.log exists and adheres strictly to the
         four-line format described above, with a correct SHA-256 digest
         matching the current contents of /home/user/firewall_update.sh.

    NOTE: Because you do not have root privileges, you must NOT attempt to
    run the iptables commands themselves—only prepare the script and log.
truth:
<!-- Ground-truth data used by the automated checker.  DO NOT reveal -->
    <script_path>/home/user/firewall_update.sh</script_path>
    <script_mode>755</script_mode>

    <!-- The three precise command lines (order is important). -->
    <cmd1>iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22   -j ACCEPT</cmd1>
    <cmd2>iptables -A INPUT -p icmp             -j ACCEPT</cmd2>
    <cmd3>iptables -A INPUT                    -j DROP</cmd3>

    <!-- Log file specification -->
    <log_path>/home/user/firewall_config.log</log_path>
    <log_lines>4</log_lines>
    <log_line2_literal>COMMANDS:3</log_line2_literal>
    <log_line3_literal>ICMP:ALLOWED</log_line3_literal>
    <log_line4_literal>DEFAULT:DROP</log_line4_literal>

    <!-- The checker will dynamically compute the expected SHA-256 digest
         from the contents of /home/user/firewall_update.sh and compare it
         to the first line of the log. -->
name: task_000000_bb98f7eb
